---
title: "Curvature and distortion: differential geometry on meshes"
---

Curvature measures how a single surface bends; distortion measures how a warp
between two surfaces stretches one onto the other. Both quantities fall out of the
operators built in the preceding chapters, and both are governed by the symmetry
theme of this unit: a good curvature estimate depends only on the *shape* of the
surface, not on how it is positioned or triangulated, and the deepest fact about
curvature - the Theorema Egregium - is itself a statement about which
transformations leave it unchanged.

This chapter develops four routines, all in
[`nitrix.geometry.surface`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/geometry/surface.py)
and built on the operators of
[`nitrix.sparse.mesh`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/sparse/mesh.py):

- **mean curvature** $H$, the Laplacian of the coordinate field. The same
  $\Delta = M^{-1}L$ from [the Laplace--Beltrami chapter](laplace-beltrami.qmd#sec-cotangent),
  applied to the vertex positions $v$ rather than to a scalar, returns a vector
  along the surface normal with length proportional to $H$, so
  $H_{\text{vec}}=\tfrac12 M^{-1}L\,v$ and $H=\operatorname{sign}(H_{\text{vec}}\!\cdot
  n_i)\,\lVert H_{\text{vec}}\rVert$;
- **Gaussian curvature** $K$, an angle defect: flatten the triangles around a vertex
  and the angles fail to close by $2\pi$; that gap, per unit area, is $K$;
- **distortion** between two meshes in correspondence: areal,
  $\log_2(A_{\text{warped}}/A_{\text{source}})$ per vertex (the surface analogue of a
  Jacobian determinant), and strain, the per-face principal stretches from the
  deformation gradient.

Everything is per-vertex or per-face, pure JAX, and differentiable with respect to
the vertices, and is validated not by bit-parity but against analytic oracles - the
sphere's exact $H=1/r$, $K=1/r^2$ - and the global Gauss--Bonnet identity
$\sum_i K_i A_i = 2\pi\chi = 4\pi$ for a genus-0 surface.

The chapter follows [foundations](foundations.qmd#sec-discretization),
[the sparse operator substrate](operators.qmd#sec-ell),
[the Laplace--Beltrami operator](laplace-beltrami.qmd#sec-cotangent), and
[the surface spectrum](spectral.qmd#sec-eigen). It assumes linear algebra -
eigenvalues of a $2\times2$ matrix, determinant and trace, quadratic forms - but no
prior differential geometry; each quantity is built from a picture first and a
formula second.

## A motivating problem {#sec-motivation}

A reconstructed cortical surface - a triangle mesh of one hemisphere - raises three
questions. *Where are the gyral crowns and the sulcal fundi?* That is curvature: the
crowns bulge outward, the fundi curl inward, and the sign and magnitude of the
bending distinguish them. This is the geometry behind FreeSurfer's `?h.curv` map, the
feature surface registrars align on. *Is the reconstruction topologically a sphere, or
did the segmentation punch a handle through it?* That is Gaussian curvature integrated
over the whole surface - a single number, $4\pi$, that holds for any genus-0 surface
no matter how folded, and that deviates the moment a handle appears. *After warping
the surface to a template, how badly did the warp stretch the cortex?* That is
distortion - the quantity a registration's areal regulariser penalises and the
`?h.jacobian` QA readout reports.

All three are differential-geometry questions, and all three reduce to operators
already in hand. Mean curvature is the Laplace--Beltrami operator applied to the
coordinate field; Gaussian curvature is a sum of triangle angles; distortion is a
ratio of vertex areas, or the eigenvalues of a tiny $2\times2$ matrix per triangle. We
return to the cortical surface in @sec-validation.

The governing idea of the unit is in force. A triangle mesh is a discrete
approximation of a smooth surface, and the craft is to define curvature on the mesh so
that it converges to the smooth truth under refinement, and to check that convergence
against cases where the answer is known. Curvature is where the discipline pays off
most visibly, because curvature is a *second-order* quantity - it depends on how
normals change, not only on positions - and second-order quantities on a
piecewise-flat mesh, which has literally zero curvature on the interior of every flat
triangle, are subtle. The resolution, in both the mean and Gaussian cases, is to push
the curvature onto the *vertices and edges*, where the mesh is not flat, and to
integrate it against the right area measure.

## Mean curvature {#sec-mean}

### Mean curvature as the Laplacian of position

Begin with the one-dimensional picture. Take a curve in the plane and a point on it.
If the curve is straight there, a small step left and a small step right land
symmetrically about the point - the average of the two neighbours *is* the point. If
the curve bends, the average of the neighbours pulls off to one side, toward the
centre of the circle the curve is hugging. The vector from the point to the average of
its neighbours points along the inward normal, and its length grows with how tightly
the curve bends. That displacement-to-the-neighbour-average is exactly what a Laplacian
computes, so the curvature of the curve is, up to a constant, the Laplacian of the
curve's *position*.

Lift this to a surface in three dimensions. The role of "neighbour average minus
point" is played by the Laplace--Beltrami operator $\Delta = M^{-1}L$ from the
[previous chapter](laplace-beltrami.qmd#sec-cotangent), applied not to a scalar field
painted on the surface but to the coordinate field $v$ itself - the three functions
$x(p), y(p), z(p)$ that say where each surface point sits in space. The result $\Delta
v$ is a *vector* at each vertex. A classical theorem of differential geometry says this
vector is normal to the surface and its length is twice the mean curvature:

$$
\Delta v \;=\; -2\,H\,n,
\qquad H = \tfrac12(\kappa_1 + \kappa_2),
$$ {#eq-mc-continuous}

where $n$ is the unit surface normal and $\kappa_1, \kappa_2$ are the principal
curvatures - the most and least bent directions at the point (the radii of the
tightest and loosest tangent circles, inverted and signed). The mean curvature $H$ is
their average and controls the magnitude of $\Delta v$. The intuition is the same as in
one dimension: $\Delta v$ is position minus neighbour average, the neighbour average
sits toward the bulk of the surface, so the vector points along the normal, and a more
sharply bent surface pulls its neighbours harder.

A word on what kind of quantity $H$ is, because it sets up the symmetry story of this
chapter. Mean curvature is *extrinsic*: it is defined through the surface normal $n$,
which is information about how the surface sits in the ambient space. It is invariant to
rigid motion of the embedding - translate or rotate the surface and $H$ at each
material point is unchanged - and equivariant to vertex relabelling, but it is *not*
invariant under bending. Roll a flat sheet of paper into a cylinder and you have changed
$H$ from $0$ to $1/(2r)$ without stretching anything. Hold this contrast in mind: it is
exactly what distinguishes $H$ from the Gaussian curvature of @sec-gaussian, which *is*
invariant under bending.

### From the smooth statement to the discrete formula

On a mesh, $\Delta = M^{-1}L$ is the cotangent stiffness $L$ divided row-wise by the
lumped mass $M=\operatorname{diag}(A_i)$ (the
[vertex areas](laplace-beltrami.qmd#sec-mass)). Writing @eq-mc-continuous per vertex
with the cotangent weights $w_{ij}=\tfrac12(\cot\alpha_{ij}+\cot\beta_{ij})$ gives the
discrete mean-curvature normal of Meyer et al. (2003):

$$
H_{\text{vec}}(v_i) \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,(M^{-1}L\,v)[i]
\;=\; \frac{1}{2 A_i}\sum_{j\in\mathcal N(i)}
w_{ij}\,(v_i - v_j).
$$ {#eq-mc-discrete}

Read the right-hand side as a force balance. Each neighbour $v_j$ pulls $v_i$ toward
itself with a stiffness $w_{ij}$ set by the *shape* of the two triangles on edge
$(i,j)$; the net pull, divided by the vertex's area $A_i$, is the mean-curvature
vector. On a flat patch the pulls cancel and $H_{\text{vec}}=0$, as they must.

The vector $H_{\text{vec}}$ carries both the magnitude and the direction of bending. To
obtain a signed scalar, project it onto the outward vertex normal $n_i$ and take the
magnitude:

$$
H_i \;=\; \operatorname{sign}\!\big(H_{\text{vec}}(v_i)\cdot n_i\big)\,
\big\lVert H_{\text{vec}}(v_i)\big\rVert.
$$ {#eq-mc-sign}

This is what `mean_curvature(mesh, *, area_scheme='voronoi')` computes **(DIFF-JAX)**.
Internally it (1) applies the cotangent stiffness to the vertex positions,
`_cotangent_apply(mesh.vertices, mesh.faces, mesh.vertices)`, to get $Lv$; (2) divides
by `vertex_areas(mesh, scheme=area_scheme)` (clamped below at `1e-12` to avoid a
divide-by-zero on degenerate vertices) and halves to form $H_{\text{vec}}$; (3) gets
$n_i$ from `compute_vertex_normals`; and (4) returns the signed magnitude of
@eq-mc-sign. It refuses an empty, faceless mesh up front (the internal `_require_faces`
guard), because a `marching_cubes` call that found no level set can legitimately return
an empty `Mesh`, and feeding that to a curvature op would silently produce all-zero
garbage rather than an error.

### A JAX-native cotangent apply, not the host-built ELL

The [previous chapter](laplace-beltrami.qmd#sec-cotangent) built the cotangent
Laplacian as an ELL operator via `mesh_cotangent_laplacian` **(HOST-CTOR)** - a sparse
matrix assembled host-side in NumPy, with the cotangent weights baked in as constants.
That is the right tool when a *fixed* operator is applied to many fields, as in
smoothing: build $L$ once, reuse it.

Mean curvature applies $L$ to the vertices *themselves*, and we want the result
differentiable with respect to those vertices - for surface optimisation, learned
refinement, or any gradient that flows back to the geometry. If the weights $w_{ij}$
are frozen NumPy constants, the gradient $\partial H/\partial v$ misses the fact that
moving a vertex changes the cotangent weights too, since the weights are themselves a
function of the geometry. So `mean_curvature` does not call the host ELL. It calls a
private JAX-native per-face cotangent apply, `_cotangent_apply`, which recomputes the
half-cotangent of each triangle angle from the live vertex positions
($\cot\theta = (u\cdot w)/\lVert u\times w\rVert$, floored at `1e-12` in the
denominator) and scatters the per-face contributions onto the vertices with
`.at[...].add(...)`. Every weight is now a differentiable function of $v$, so the
gradient is exact.

This is a recurring pattern in the suite - differentiate through the geometry, not only
through the field - and it carries a cost: the two paths must agree. The host-built ELL
and the JAX-native apply compute the *same operator* by *different code*, and they are
regression-tested to agree to about `1e-4` (a float32 assembly-order tolerance, not
bit-parity - the [class/correlation posture](foundations.qmd#sec-validation) again). The
rule from [foundations](foundations.qmd#sec-classes) applies: never call a HOST-CTOR op
inside a jitted loop. `_cotangent_apply` is pure JAX and jits and vmaps freely;
`mesh_cotangent_laplacian` builds on the host and must be hoisted out.

### The sign convention, and why it is the opposite of FreeSurfer

The sign in @eq-mc-sign is a choice, and `nitrix` makes the convex-positive one: $H_i$
is positive where the surface is convex with respect to its outward normal - a sphere,
a gyral crown - and negative in concave regions, such as a sulcal fundus. This follows
directly from the outward-normal / mean-curvature-vector definition with no extra flip,
which is why it was chosen. It is also exactly the orientation-dependence flagged in
[foundations](foundations.qmd#sec-transformations-bias): mean curvature is invariant
under proper rigid motions but sign-flips under a reflection, so a sign convention must
be fixed by naming the normal's orientation.

It is, deliberately, the opposite sign to FreeSurfer's `?h.curv`, which is positive in
sulci. There is no contradiction and no bug; it is a convention, and consumers who need
the FreeSurfer sign negate $H$. We return to the empirical consequences in
@sec-validation, where the anti-correlation with `?h.curv` turns out to be a feature,
fully accounted for, not a defect.

```{python}
#| label: fig-curvature-mean
#| fig-cap: "Mean curvature on analytic meshes. Left: an icosphere is uniformly convex, $H\\approx 1/r=1$ everywhere (the analytic oracle). Right: a torus bends outward on its outer equator ($H>0$) and curls concavely on the inner ring ($H<0$). The colour is the convex-positive $H$ of @eq-mc-sign, reproduced here in pure numpy."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from mpl_toolkits.mplot3d.art3d import Poly3DCollection

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)


def icosphere(n_sub):
    t = (1 + np.sqrt(5)) / 2
    V = np.array([
        [-1, t, 0], [1, t, 0], [-1, -t, 0], [1, -t, 0],
        [0, -1, t], [0, 1, t], [0, -1, -t], [0, 1, -t],
        [t, 0, -1], [t, 0, 1], [-t, 0, -1], [-t, 0, 1],
    ], float)
    V /= np.linalg.norm(V, axis=1, keepdims=True)
    F = np.array([
        [0, 11, 5], [0, 5, 1], [0, 1, 7], [0, 7, 10], [0, 10, 11],
        [1, 5, 9], [5, 11, 4], [11, 10, 2], [10, 7, 6], [7, 1, 8],
        [3, 9, 4], [3, 4, 2], [3, 2, 6], [3, 6, 8], [3, 8, 9],
        [4, 9, 5], [2, 4, 11], [6, 2, 10], [8, 6, 7], [9, 8, 1],
    ])
    for _ in range(n_sub):
        cache, Vl = {}, V.tolist()

        def mid(i, j):
            k = (min(i, j), max(i, j))
            if k in cache:
                return cache[k]
            m = (V[i] + V[j]) / 2
            m /= np.linalg.norm(m)
            cache[k] = len(Vl)
            Vl.append(m.tolist())
            return cache[k]

        nF = []
        for a, b, c in F:
            ab, bc, ca = mid(a, b), mid(b, c), mid(c, a)
            nF += [[a, ab, ca], [b, bc, ab], [c, ca, bc], [ab, bc, ca]]
        V = np.array(Vl)
        F = np.array(nF)
    return V, F


def torus(nu=40, nv=20, R=1.0, r=0.35):
    u = np.linspace(0, 2 * np.pi, nu, endpoint=False)
    v = np.linspace(0, 2 * np.pi, nv, endpoint=False)
    U, Vv = np.meshgrid(u, v, indexing='ij')
    x = (R + r * np.cos(Vv)) * np.cos(U)
    y = (R + r * np.cos(Vv)) * np.sin(U)
    z = r * np.sin(Vv)
    P = np.stack([x, y, z], -1).reshape(-1, 3)
    F = []
    for i in range(nu):
        for j in range(nv):
            a = i * nv + j
            b = ((i + 1) % nu) * nv + j
            c = ((i + 1) % nu) * nv + (j + 1) % nv
            d = i * nv + (j + 1) % nv
            F += [[a, b, c], [a, c, d]]
    return P, np.array(F)


def vertex_normals(V, F):
    fn = np.cross(V[F[:, 1]] - V[F[:, 0]], V[F[:, 2]] - V[F[:, 0]])
    N = np.zeros_like(V)
    for k in range(3):
        np.add.at(N, F[:, k], fn)
    return N / np.maximum(np.linalg.norm(N, axis=1, keepdims=True), 1e-12)


def bary_areas(V, F):
    A2 = np.linalg.norm(np.cross(V[F[:, 1]] - V[F[:, 0]],
                                 V[F[:, 2]] - V[F[:, 0]]), axis=1)
    area = np.zeros(len(V))
    for k in range(3):
        np.add.at(area, F[:, k], A2 / 6.0)
    return area


def cot_apply(V, F, field):  # the numpy twin of _cotangent_apply
    a, b, c = V[F[:, 0]], V[F[:, 1]], V[F[:, 2]]
    fa, fb, fc = field[F[:, 0]], field[F[:, 1]], field[F[:, 2]]

    def hc(u, w):
        cr = np.sqrt(np.sum(np.cross(u, w) ** 2, axis=1))
        return (0.5 * np.sum(u * w, axis=1) / np.maximum(cr, 1e-12))[:, None]

    ca, cb, cc = hc(b - a, c - a), hc(c - b, a - b), hc(a - c, b - c)
    out = np.zeros_like(field)
    np.add.at(out, F[:, 1], ca * (fb - fc)); np.add.at(out, F[:, 2], ca * (fc - fb))
    np.add.at(out, F[:, 2], cb * (fc - fa)); np.add.at(out, F[:, 0], cb * (fa - fc))
    np.add.at(out, F[:, 0], cc * (fa - fb)); np.add.at(out, F[:, 1], cc * (fb - fa))
    return out


def mean_curv(V, F):
    Lv = cot_apply(V, F, V)
    A = np.maximum(bary_areas(V, F), 1e-12)
    Hvec = 0.5 * Lv / A[:, None]
    N = vertex_normals(V, F)
    return np.sign(np.sum(Hvec * N, axis=1)) * np.linalg.norm(Hvec, axis=1)


fig = plt.figure(figsize=(11, 5))
for idx, (name, (V, F)) in enumerate(
    [("sphere (H = 1/r = 1)", icosphere(3)),
     ("torus (inner ring is concave)", torus())]
):
    H = mean_curv(V, F)
    ax = fig.add_subplot(1, 2, idx + 1, projection='3d')
    fc = H[F].mean(axis=1)
    lim = np.percentile(np.abs(fc), 98) + 1e-9
    coll = Poly3DCollection(V[F], cmap='RdBu_r', edgecolor='none')
    coll.set_array(fc)
    coll.set_clim(-lim, lim)
    ax.add_collection3d(coll)
    r = np.abs(V).max()
    ax.set_xlim(-r, r); ax.set_ylim(-r, r); ax.set_zlim(-r, r)
    ax.set_box_aspect((1, 1, 1)); ax.set_axis_off()
    ax.set_title(f"{name}\nmean H in [{H.min():.2f}, {H.max():.2f}]")
    fig.colorbar(coll, ax=ax, shrink=0.55, label="mean curvature H")
fig.suptitle("Mean curvature: sphere uniform 1/r; torus inner ring concave (H<0)")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

In @fig-curvature-mean the icosphere comes back uniformly positive at very nearly
$1/r=1$ - the analytic oracle the implementation is tested against, where
`mean_curvature` returns `H = 1.0000` to plotting precision. (The figure uses the cheap
barycentric area for brevity; the shipped default is the obtuse-safe Voronoi area of
@sec-distortion-areas, which matters on real cortex but not on these near-regular
meshes.) The torus shows both signs: convex on the outer equator, concave on the inner
ring where the surface curls back on itself - a first taste of the saddle geometry
quantified exactly with $K$ next.

## Gaussian curvature and Gauss--Bonnet {#sec-gaussian}

### Curvature as the angle that fails to close

Mean curvature measures how much the surface bends; Gaussian curvature measures how it
bends in two directions at once, and it has a purely intrinsic characterisation that
needs no normal vector at all. Cut a paper cone along a straight line from tip to rim
and flatten it onto a table. It lies flat, but there is a wedge of missing angle: the
flattened cone does not sweep a full $360°$ around the former tip. A flat sheet sweeps
exactly $360°$; a cone sweeps less; a saddle-shaped chip sweeps more, buckling because
it has too much angle to lie flat. That surplus or deficit of angle, concentrated at
the tip, *is* the Gaussian curvature gathered up at that point.

Make it precise on a mesh. Around an interior vertex $v_i$ sit some triangles, each
contributing an interior angle $\theta_{ij}$ at $v_i$. If the surface were flat there,
those angles would tile the plane around $v_i$ and sum to exactly $2\pi$. The amount by
which they fail to reach $2\pi$ is the angle defect, and the angle defect is the
integrated Gaussian curvature in the neighbourhood of $v_i$. Dividing by the vertex's
area $A_i$ turns the integrated quantity into a pointwise density:

$$
K(v_i) \;=\; \frac{2\pi - \sum_{j} \theta_{ij}}{A_i}.
$$ {#eq-gauss-defect}

The numerator is the angle defect; the denominator is the vertex area. A flat vertex
has $\sum\theta_{ij}=2\pi$ and $K=0$; a convex bulge (a cone tip, a sphere) has
$\sum\theta_{ij}<2\pi$ and $K>0$; a saddle has $\sum\theta_{ij}>2\pi$ and $K<0$. This is
exactly the cone-flattening picture, and it is what `gaussian_curvature(mesh, *,
area_scheme='voronoi')` computes **(DIFF-JAX)**.

The defining feature of @eq-gauss-defect is that it is built entirely from angles and
areas - lengths measured *within* the surface - and never refers to the normal or the
embedding. Gaussian curvature is therefore *intrinsic*, and this is the content of
Gauss's Theorema Egregium ("remarkable theorem"): $K$ is invariant under any isometry,
any bending of the surface that preserves distances measured along it. A flat sheet has
$K=0$; roll it into a cylinder and, although the mean curvature jumps, $K$ stays $0$
everywhere, which is why a cylinder can be cut and unrolled flat without stretching but
a sphere ($K=1/r^2>0$) cannot - the root cause of every map projection's distortion.
This is the strongest invariance in the chapter: where mean curvature is invariant only
to rigid motion, Gaussian curvature is invariant to the entire group of isometric
deformations, and a computation built on $K$ inherits that invariance for free.

### Why `arctan2`, not `arccos`

A detail in the as-built code repays attention. The interior angle between two edges
$u$ and $w$ is the angle of a triangle, and the textbook formula is
$\theta = \arccos\!\big((u\cdot w)/(\lVert u\rVert\,\lVert w\rVert)\big)$. `nitrix` does
not use `arccos`. It uses

$$
\theta \;=\; \operatorname{atan2}\!\big(\lVert u\times w\rVert,\; u\cdot w\big),
$$ {#eq-angle-atan2}

the two-argument arctangent of the cross-product magnitude against the dot product.
Algebraically the two agree; numerically they do not. $\arccos$ has infinite derivative
at its endpoints $\pm 1$, and after the argument is clipped into $[-1,1]$ (which it must
be, to survive float rounding) the gradient there is *zeroed* instead. Real cortical
surfaces - especially the sliver triangles that marching-tetrahedra output produces,
see [the volume-surface chapter](volume-surface.qmd#sec-marching) - routinely have
near-collinear edges sitting right at those endpoints. The `atan2` form is smooth and
well-conditioned across the whole range, so the curvature stays differentiable even on
degenerate triangles. This is the same robustness instinct as the discriminant flooring
in @sec-distortion-strain: keep the gradient finite exactly where the naive formula
would blow up.

### Gauss--Bonnet: geometry meets topology

Because the numerator of @eq-gauss-defect is the integrated $K$ (an angle defect, not a
density), summing $K_i A_i$ over the mesh reduces to summing the angle defects, and a
classical theorem says that total angle defect depends only on the surface's topology,
not its shape:

$$
\sum_i K_i A_i \;=\; 2\pi\,\chi \;=\; 4\pi \quad(\text{genus-0}),
$$ {#eq-gauss-bonnet}

where $\chi = V - E + F$ is the Euler characteristic ($\chi = 2$ for any surface
topologically a sphere, $\chi=0$ for a torus, $\chi=2-2g$ for genus $g$). This is the
discrete Gauss--Bonnet theorem, and on a mesh it holds exactly - not in the limit, not
approximately, but to floating-point at every refinement level - precisely because the
angle-defect numerator is integrated and the sum of all interior angles over all
triangles is a combinatorial constant. (Continuous Gauss--Bonnet is $\int_S K\,dA =
2\pi\chi$; the discrete version is its faithful finite shadow.)

Read through the lens of symmetry, Gauss--Bonnet is the most extreme invariance in the
unit. Pointwise Gaussian curvature is invariant under isometries; its integral is
invariant under *all* homeomorphisms - any continuous deformation whatever, isometric
or not - because it equals a topological invariant. This is why the check is so
powerful in practice. Compute $\sum_i K_i A_i$ on a genus-0 cortical surface and the
answer must be $4\pi$; anything else means either the mesh is not genus-0 (a handle
from a segmentation error) or the areas and angles are inconsistent. The Euler
characteristic and genus themselves are computed on
[the volume-surface chapter](volume-surface.qmd#sec-topology); here Gauss--Bonnet lets
us cross-check topology purely from geometry.

```{python}
#| label: fig-curvature-gaussbonnet
#| fig-cap: "Discrete Gauss–Bonnet is exact at every refinement level. The total integrated Gaussian curvature $\\sum_i K_i A_i$ (the sum of angle defects, @eq-gauss-defect) over an icosphere lands on $4\\pi$ at every subdivision level from the 12-vertex icosahedron to ~10k vertices — the residual is float round-off, not discretisation error. This is the discretisation-is-a-faithful-shadow thesis made visual: a topological invariant captured exactly by a sum of triangle angles."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)


def icosphere(n_sub):
    t = (1 + np.sqrt(5)) / 2
    V = np.array([
        [-1, t, 0], [1, t, 0], [-1, -t, 0], [1, -t, 0],
        [0, -1, t], [0, 1, t], [0, -1, -t], [0, 1, -t],
        [t, 0, -1], [t, 0, 1], [-t, 0, -1], [-t, 0, 1],
    ], float)
    V /= np.linalg.norm(V, axis=1, keepdims=True)
    F = np.array([
        [0, 11, 5], [0, 5, 1], [0, 1, 7], [0, 7, 10], [0, 10, 11],
        [1, 5, 9], [5, 11, 4], [11, 10, 2], [10, 7, 6], [7, 1, 8],
        [3, 9, 4], [3, 4, 2], [3, 2, 6], [3, 6, 8], [3, 8, 9],
        [4, 9, 5], [2, 4, 11], [6, 2, 10], [8, 6, 7], [9, 8, 1],
    ])
    for _ in range(n_sub):
        cache, Vl = {}, V.tolist()

        def mid(i, j):
            k = (min(i, j), max(i, j))
            if k in cache:
                return cache[k]
            m = (V[i] + V[j]) / 2
            m /= np.linalg.norm(m)
            cache[k] = len(Vl)
            Vl.append(m.tolist())
            return cache[k]

        nF = []
        for a, b, c in F:
            ab, bc, ca = mid(a, b), mid(b, c), mid(c, a)
            nF += [[a, ab, ca], [b, bc, ab], [c, ca, bc], [ab, bc, ca]]
        V = np.array(Vl)
        F = np.array(nF)
    return V, F


def total_angle_defect(V, F):  # = sum_i K_i A_i ; angles via atan2 (@eq-angle-atan2)
    a, b, c = V[F[:, 0]], V[F[:, 1]], V[F[:, 2]]

    def ang(u, w):
        cr = np.sqrt(np.sum(np.cross(u, w) ** 2, axis=1))
        return np.arctan2(cr, np.sum(u * w, axis=1))

    asum = np.zeros(len(V))
    np.add.at(asum, F[:, 0], ang(b - a, c - a))
    np.add.at(asum, F[:, 1], ang(a - b, c - b))
    np.add.at(asum, F[:, 2], ang(a - c, b - c))
    return np.sum(2 * np.pi - asum)


levels = list(range(0, 6))
nverts, totals = [], []
for L in levels:
    V, F = icosphere(L)
    nverts.append(len(V))
    totals.append(total_angle_defect(V, F))

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4.5))
ax.axhline(4 * np.pi, color='k', ls='--', lw=1,
           label=r'$4\pi$ (Gauss-Bonnet, $\chi=2$)')
ax.plot(nverts, totals, 'o-', color='C3',
        label=r'$\sum_i K_i A_i$ (sum of angle defects)')
for x, y in zip(nverts, totals):
    ax.annotate(f'{y - 4 * np.pi:+.1e}', (x, y), textcoords='offset points',
                xytext=(0, 8), ha='center', fontsize=7)
ax.set_xscale('log')
ax.set_xlabel('vertices (icosphere subdivision level 0..5)')
ax.set_ylabel(r'$\sum_i K_i A_i$')
ax.set_ylim(4 * np.pi - 0.02, 4 * np.pi + 0.02)
ax.set_title('Discrete Gauss-Bonnet is EXACT at every refinement level')
ax.legend()
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

@fig-curvature-gaussbonnet shows the residual $\sum_i K_i A_i - 4\pi$ holding at the
$10^{-6}$ to $10^{-5}$ level - pure float round-off - across two and a half orders of
magnitude in vertex count. This is not convergence-under-refinement in the usual sense,
where a discrete quantity creeps toward its continuous target as the mesh fines; it is
the rarer and stronger statement that the discrete identity is exactly true at every
level, because Gauss--Bonnet is combinatorial. Pointwise $K_i$ on a coarse mesh is a
rough estimate of the smooth $K$; their area-weighted sum is nailed.

### Principal curvatures from $H$ and $K$

The two principal curvatures $\kappa_1\ge\kappa_2$ are the most- and least-bent
directions, and they need no separate eigen-analysis: mean and Gaussian curvature
already determine the pair. By definition $H=\tfrac12(\kappa_1 + \kappa_2)$ is their
average and $K=\kappa_1\kappa_2$ is their product, and two numbers known by sum and
product are the roots of a quadratic, so

$$
\kappa_{1,2} \;=\; H \pm \sqrt{\max(H^2 - K,\,0)}.
$$ {#eq-principal}

`principal_curvatures(mesh, *, area_scheme='voronoi')` **(DIFF-JAX)** computes exactly
this: it calls `mean_curvature` and `gaussian_curvature`, forms the discriminant
$H^2-K$, and returns the stacked pair `(kappa_1, kappa_2)` with column 0 the larger.
The discriminant is clamped at zero before the square root because on a discrete mesh
$H^2-K$ can dip slightly negative from estimation error (the exact relation $H^2\ge K$
holds for the smooth surface; the two discrete estimators do not enforce it pointwise).
Clamping keeps the output real and the gradient finite, at the cost of collapsing the
pair to $\kappa_1=\kappa_2=H$ exactly where the estimate would have been complex - a
conservative choice. The sign convention is inherited from `mean_curvature`
(convex-positive).

A torus makes the geometry vivid if you extend @fig-curvature-mean: on the outer equator
both principal curvatures bend the same way and $K>0$; on the inner ring the surface
saddles, the two principal curvatures have opposite signs, and $K<0$ - exactly the angle
surplus of the cone picture, now read off the sign of the product.

## Distortion: what a warp does to a surface {#sec-distortion}

Curvature is a property of one surface. Distortion compares *two* meshes in vertex
correspondence - the same mesh before and after a warp - and asks how the warp stretched
the cortex. This is the bridge from differential geometry to registration QA: when a
subject's surface is aligned to a template, a good warp distorts the surface as little
as possible, and distortion is both the quantity a registration's areal regulariser
penalises and the quantity a `?h.jacobian` overlay reports. There are two flavours, at
two granularities: an areal measure per vertex and a strain measure per face.

Both require the two meshes to share topology - identical `faces`, with vertex $i$ of
`source` corresponding to vertex $i$ of `warped`. This holds exactly when `warped` is
`source` after a warp that moves vertices but never re-tessellates, such as a spherical
or inflation warp, or the output of
[`deform_to_sdf`](volume-surface.qmd#sec-mesh-sdf). Both routines validate it by
comparing `faces.shape` and raise `ValueError` on a mismatch. Both measures are
invariant to a rigid motion applied to either mesh - they depend only on relative
lengths and areas, not on pose - which is the inductive bias a distortion regulariser
should have: it must penalise stretching, never the arbitrary placement of source or
target in space.

### Areal distortion: the surface Jacobian determinant {#sec-distortion-areas}

The simplest question is whether the warp expanded or shrank the surface near each
vertex. Compare the vertex area before and after. The natural scale is logarithmic - a
doubling and a halving should be equal and opposite, not lopsided - and base-2 gives
readable units, one unit per doubling, so

$$
D_{\text{areal}}(v_i) \;=\; \log_2\!\frac{A_{\text{warped}}(v_i)}
{A_{\text{source}}(v_i)},
$$ {#eq-areal}

which is $0$ for an isometry (areas unchanged), positive where the warp expands area,
negative where it contracts. This is the surface analogue of a Jacobian determinant -
the volumetric `jacobian_det_displacement` and the MSM areal regulariser report the same
thing in their own domains. `areal_distortion(source, warped, *,
area_scheme='voronoi')` **(DIFF-JAX)** computes it directly: `vertex_areas` on each mesh
(clamped below at `1e-12`), the log2 ratio, done. It is differentiable with respect to
*both* meshes' vertices, so it can sit inside a registration loss.

This is also the moment to treat the area measure properly, because it is load-bearing.
`vertex_areas(mesh, *, scheme='voronoi')` **(DIFF-JAX)** partitions each triangle's area
among its three vertices, and offers two schemes that both sum to the total surface
area:

- **`'barycentric'`** gives each vertex one-third of every incident triangle's area.
  Cheap, always non-negative, the safe fallback.
- **`'voronoi'`** (the default) uses the mixed Voronoi rule of Meyer et al. (2003): for
  a non-obtuse triangle, vertex $i$ receives the circumcentric-dual area
  $\tfrac18\big(\cot\theta_k\,\lVert x_i-x_j\rVert^2 + \cot\theta_j\,\lVert
  x_i-x_k\rVert^2\big)$ - the region of the triangle genuinely closest to $v_i$. For an
  obtuse triangle the circumcentre falls outside the triangle, so the Voronoi area would
  be wrong, and the mixed rule instead assigns half the triangle area to the obtuse
  vertex and a quarter to each of the others.

That obtuse branch is load-bearing on real cortical surfaces, where obtuse triangles
are common, and is exactly what makes Voronoi the right default; the icosphere never
exercises it, which is why @fig-curvature-mean could use the barycentric area. The same
`vertex_areas` is the lumped mass $M$ behind mean curvature, geodesic smoothing, and the
spectrum - one area measure threaded through the whole unit. For completeness, the
per-face area $\tfrac12\lVert(v_1-v_0)\times(v_2-v_0)\rVert$ is available directly as
`face_areas(mesh)` **(DIFF-JAX)**, and the diagonal mass operator itself as
`mesh_mass_matrix(mesh, *, scheme='voronoi', lumped=True)` **(HOST-CTOR)** - a width-1
ELL wrapping the same `vertex_areas` vector so $M$ can flow through the `apply_operator`
seam; `lumped=False` (the consistent FEM mass) raises `NotImplementedError`.

### Strain distortion: principal stretches from the deformation gradient {#sec-distortion-strain}

Areal distortion sees only the determinant of the warp's local action - total area
change. It cannot tell a uniform stretch from an anisotropic shear that preserves area.
For the full shape change the right object is the per-triangle deformation gradient: the
linear map that carries the source triangle onto the warped one. Its singular values are
the principal stretches $\lambda_1\ge\lambda_2$ - how much the triangle is stretched
along its two principal directions. An isometry gives $(1,1)$; a uniform scale $s$ gives
$(s,s)$; an anisotropic scale $\operatorname{diag}(a,b)$ gives
$(\max(a,b),\min(a,b))$.

The clean way to obtain these without ever building a $3\times3\to2\times2$ basis is
through the first fundamental forms of the two triangles. For a triangle with edge
vectors $e_1, e_2$ from its first vertex, the first fundamental form is the $2\times2$
Gram matrix $G = \begin{pmatrix} e_1\!\cdot e_1 & e_1\!\cdot e_2 \\ e_1\!\cdot e_2 &
e_2\!\cdot e_2 \end{pmatrix}$, which encodes all intrinsic lengths and angles of the
triangle. The right Cauchy--Green tensor $C = G_s^{-1} G_w$ relates the source and
warped forms, and its eigenvalues are the squared principal stretches, so

$$
\lambda^2 \;=\; \frac{\operatorname{tr}C \pm
\sqrt{(\operatorname{tr}C)^2 - 4\det C}}{2},
\qquad C = G_s^{-1} G_w.
$$ {#eq-strain}

This is the standard $2\times2$ eigenvalue closed form - sum and product of eigenvalues
are $\operatorname{tr}C$ and $\det C$, the quadratic formula again, the same move as
@eq-principal. Because the construction uses only the Gram matrices, which are built
from edge dot products, the stretches are automatically invariant to a rotation of
either triangle in space; they measure shape change, not pose. `strain_distortion(source,
warped)` **(DIFF-JAX)** computes it without ever calling `eigh`: it forms the six Gram
entries $g_{11},g_{12},g_{22}$ (source) and $w_{11},w_{12},w_{22}$ (warped) directly
from edge dot products, evaluates $\operatorname{tr}C$ and $\det C$ in closed form from
those entries and $\det G_s$, and takes square roots to return the stretches
$(\lambda_1,\lambda_2)$. Two robustness details earn their place:

- **No `eigh`.** Using the closed form rather than a $2\times2$ eigensolver bypasses the
  cuSolver wedge - the host-synchronising eigendecomposition path that `nitrix` works
  hard to avoid on GPU (see [the solvers chapter](solvers.qmd#sec-eigensolve)). A
  per-face $2\times2$ eigenvalue is a handful of arithmetic operations; routing it
  through `eigh` would force a device-to-host round trip per call.
- **Floored discriminant.** $(\operatorname{tr}C)^2-4\det C$ is floored at `1e-12`
  before the square root. At the crossing $\lambda_1=\lambda_2$ the discriminant is
  genuinely zero and $\sqrt{\,\cdot\,}$ is non-differentiable there; flooring keeps the
  gradient finite, at the cost of splitting genuinely equal stretches by about `1e-6`.
  So an isometry comes back as $(1,1)$ only to `1e-6` - far below the `~5e-4` float32
  cancellation noise that near-degenerate stretches already carry, and the same
  defensive instinct as the `atan2` angle in @sec-gaussian. Distinct stretches are
  well-conditioned and unaffected.

```{python}
#| label: fig-curvature-distortion
#| fig-cap: "Distortion on a flat triangulated patch under known warps recovers the closed-form answer. Left: a uniform scale $s=1.5$ gives areal distortion $\\log_2 s^2$ everywhere (the dashed line). Middle: the same warp gives both strain stretches $\\lambda_1=\\lambda_2=s$. Right: an anisotropic warp $\\operatorname{diag}(2,\\tfrac12)$ gives strains $(\\lambda_1,\\lambda_2)=(2,\\tfrac12)$ — area-preserving (the product is 1) but maximally shape-distorting, which areal distortion alone could never see."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)


def flat_grid(nx=9, ny=9):
    xs = np.linspace(0, 1, nx)
    ys = np.linspace(0, 1, ny)
    X, Y = np.meshgrid(xs, ys, indexing='ij')
    V = np.stack([X.ravel(), Y.ravel(), np.zeros(X.size)], -1)
    F = []
    for i in range(nx - 1):
        for j in range(ny - 1):
            a = i * ny + j
            b = (i + 1) * ny + j
            c = (i + 1) * ny + j + 1
            d = i * ny + j + 1
            F += [[a, b, c], [a, c, d]]
    return V, np.array(F)


def bary_areas(V, F):
    A2 = np.linalg.norm(np.cross(V[F[:, 1]] - V[F[:, 0]],
                                 V[F[:, 2]] - V[F[:, 0]]), axis=1)
    area = np.zeros(len(V))
    for k in range(3):
        np.add.at(area, F[:, k], A2 / 6.0)
    return area


def areal_distortion(Vs, Vw, F):  # @eq-areal
    return np.log2(np.maximum(bary_areas(Vw, F), 1e-12)
                   / np.maximum(bary_areas(Vs, F), 1e-12))


def strain_distortion(Vs, Vw, F):  # @eq-strain, the numpy twin of the JAX op
    s1, s2 = Vs[F[:, 1]] - Vs[F[:, 0]], Vs[F[:, 2]] - Vs[F[:, 0]]
    t1, t2 = Vw[F[:, 1]] - Vw[F[:, 0]], Vw[F[:, 2]] - Vw[F[:, 0]]
    g11, g12, g22 = (s1 * s1).sum(1), (s1 * s2).sum(1), (s2 * s2).sum(1)
    w11, w12, w22 = (t1 * t1).sum(1), (t1 * t2).sum(1), (t2 * t2).sum(1)
    det_s = np.maximum(g11 * g22 - g12 ** 2, 1e-12)
    tr_c = (g22 * w11 - 2 * g12 * w12 + g11 * w22) / det_s
    det_c = (w11 * w22 - w12 ** 2) / det_s
    disc = np.sqrt(np.maximum(tr_c ** 2 - 4 * det_c, 1e-12))
    lam1 = np.sqrt(np.maximum((tr_c + disc) / 2, 0))
    lam2 = np.sqrt(np.maximum((tr_c - disc) / 2, 0))
    return np.stack([lam1, lam2], -1)


V, F = flat_grid()
s = 1.5
Vs1 = V.copy(); Vs1[:, :2] *= s            # uniform scale
a, b = 2.0, 0.5
Va = V.copy(); Va[:, 0] *= a; Va[:, 1] *= b  # anisotropic

ar1 = areal_distortion(V, Vs1, F)
st1 = strain_distortion(V, Vs1, F)
st2 = strain_distortion(V, Va, F)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(13, 4))

pred0 = np.log2(s ** 2)
axes[0].hist(ar1, bins=np.linspace(pred0 - 0.05, pred0 + 0.05, 21), color='C0')
axes[0].axvline(pred0, color='k', ls='--', label=f'predicted log2(s^2)={pred0:.3f}')
axes[0].set_title(f'Areal distortion, uniform scale s={s}\nmeasured mean {ar1.mean():.3f}')
axes[0].set_xlabel('log2(area ratio)'); axes[0].legend()

bins1 = np.linspace(s - 0.1, s + 0.1, 21)
axes[1].hist(st1[:, 0], bins=bins1, alpha=0.7, label='lambda_1')
axes[1].hist(st1[:, 1], bins=bins1, alpha=0.7, label='lambda_2')
axes[1].axvline(s, color='k', ls='--', label=f'predicted s={s}')
axes[1].set_title(f'Strain, uniform scale s={s}\nboth stretches ~ {st1.mean():.3f}')
axes[1].set_xlabel('principal stretch'); axes[1].legend()

bins2 = np.linspace(min(b, st2[:, 1].min()) - 0.1, max(a, st2[:, 0].max()) + 0.1, 31)
axes[2].hist(st2[:, 0], bins=bins2, alpha=0.7, label='lambda_1')
axes[2].hist(st2[:, 1], bins=bins2, alpha=0.7, label='lambda_2')
axes[2].axvline(a, color='C0', ls='--', label=f'a={a}')
axes[2].axvline(b, color='C1', ls='--', label=f'b={b}')
axes[2].set_title(f'Strain, anisotropic diag({a},{b})\n-> ({st2[:,0].mean():.2f}, {st2[:,1].mean():.2f})')
axes[2].set_xlabel('principal stretch'); axes[2].legend()

fig.suptitle('Distortion under known warps recovers the analytic answer')
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

@fig-curvature-distortion is the distortion analogue of the sphere oracle: a known warp
with a known answer, used to pin the formulas. The uniform scale returns $\log_2 s^2$
areal and $(s,s)$ strain to plotting precision; the anisotropic warp returns
$(2,\tfrac12)$ strain even though its areal distortion is zero (the product of the
stretches is $1$, so area is preserved). That last panel is the whole case for strain: a
warp can preserve area while ruining shape, and only the principal stretches catch it.

## Validation, and the FreeSurfer `?h.curv` story {#sec-validation}

The unit's [validation posture](foundations.qmd#sec-validation) is
class-and-correlation, not bit-parity, and curvature is its cleanest case study. There
are three tiers of check, in increasing order of how much they tell you.

**The analytic oracle.** A unit sphere has $H=1/r=1$ and $K=1/r^2=1$ exactly, and the
implementation reproduces both to about `1.0000` - a closed-form ground truth with no
reference tool in the loop. This is the gold standard: where an exact answer exists,
demand it.

**The global topological check.** On a real genus-0 cortical surface, $\sum_i K_i A_i =
4\pi$ by discrete Gauss--Bonnet (@eq-gauss-bonnet) - a check that needs no oracle at
all, only the surface's own geometry and topology, and that flags a handle-puncturing
reconstruction error instantly.

**The correlation against a reference tool.** Comparing nitrix mean curvature to
FreeSurfer's `?h.curv` on `fsaverage5` white surfaces gives a Pearson correlation of
about $-0.90$. The sign is negative by construction - the convex-positive convention of
@sec-mean is the opposite of FreeSurfer's. The informative number is the magnitude: why
$0.90$ and not $1.0$? The gap is fully decomposed, measured rather than assumed:

1. **FreeSurfer smooths `curv`.** `mris_curvature` averages by default, so `?h.curv` is
   a lightly smoothed curvature. Applying matched light smoothing (about two one-ring
   passes) to the nitrix $H$ lifts the correlation to $-0.95$, and then it decays with
   further smoothing - the characteristic peak-then-decay signature of "the reference is
   a smoothed version of what we compute."
2. **Different discrete estimators.** nitrix uses the cotangent / Meyer mean-curvature
   normal (@eq-mc-discrete); FreeSurfer fits a local quadric (the second fundamental
   form). Both converge to the continuous $H$ under refinement; they differ pointwise on
   a finite mesh.
3. **The overlay is group-averaged and resolution-resampled.** The `fsaverage5` `curv`
   is a cross-subject average carried down to ico5, not a curvature computed on that
   exact 10,242-vertex tessellation - the same artefact class as the `fsaverage5` `area`
   overlay summing to $0.75\times$ the geometric area.
4. **It is white-surface-specific.** Measuring $H$ on the pial surface drops the
   correlation to $-0.74$, confirming that `?h.curv` is specifically the white surface's
   curvature.

Each line is an understood source of disagreement, not a bug. This is what
class/correlation validation buys: instead of a brittle bit-parity test that breaks when
FreeSurfer changes its smoothing default, you get a *model* of the discrepancy that
explains every part of the gap from unity. The cortical surface of @sec-motivation,
then, has a curvature map that agrees with FreeSurfer in every way that geometry demands
and differs in exactly the ways the two pipelines' choices predict.

## Further reading

- Meyer, M., Desbrun, M., Schröder, P., & Barr, A. H. (2003). *Discrete
  Differential-Geometry Operators for Triangulated 2-Manifolds.* In Visualization and
  Mathematics III (Springer). The source of the cotangent mean-curvature normal and the
  mixed-Voronoi area used throughout this chapter.
- Crane, K. (2018). *Discrete Differential Geometry: An Applied Introduction.* (Lecture
  notes, CMU.) The clearest modern treatment of the angle defect, discrete
  Gauss--Bonnet, and the discrete-approximates-smooth philosophy.
- do Carmo, M. P. (1976). *Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces.* Prentice-Hall.
  The classical smooth theory: first and second fundamental forms, principal curvatures,
  the Theorema Egregium, and the Gauss--Bonnet theorem.
- Botsch, M., Kobbelt, L., Pauly, M., Alliez, P., & Lévy, B. (2010). *Polygon Mesh
  Processing.* A K Peters. Discrete curvature estimators and mesh-processing practice,
  including the practical pitfalls of curvature on irregular meshes.

## See also

- Source:
  [`geometry/surface.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/geometry/surface.py)
  (`mean_curvature`, `gaussian_curvature`, `principal_curvatures`, `areal_distortion`,
  `strain_distortion`) and
  [`sparse/mesh.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/sparse/mesh.py)
  (`vertex_areas`, `face_areas`, `compute_vertex_normals`, `mesh_mass_matrix`).
- Previous chapter: [the Laplace–Beltrami operator](laplace-beltrami.qmd#sec-cotangent),
  the $M^{-1}L$ that mean curvature is built on.
- Next: [volumes and surfaces](volume-surface.qmd#sec-topology), the Euler
  characteristic and genus that Gauss--Bonnet cross-checks, and the
  [marching-tetrahedra](volume-surface.qmd#sec-marching) meshes whose slivers motivate
  the `atan2` angle.
- Sideways: [the surface spectrum](spectral.qmd#sec-eigen), the same operator's
  eigenfunctions; [the solvers chapter](solvers.qmd#sec-eigensolve), the cuSolver wedge
  the closed-form $2\times2$ strain eigenvalue avoids.
